Catarina had terrible scoliosis. According to the doctors, her condition was incurable.  She was sentenced to spend her entire life wearing a corset.  Without hope, without an alternative.  It was either that or surgery.  But Catherine decided not to accept that fate.  At 16, she began to seek alternatives on her own.  And then she had an idea.  She observed how the balloon changed shape depending on whether it was empty or full of air.  And then she looked at her own body, realizing that the balloons are like our lungs that fill with air.  And she thought to herself, "What if I could do the same thing? But with my breath?"  That's when Catarina stood in front of the mirror.  She moved her torso, restricting airflow in one lung, breathing more deeply into the other, constantly testing and adjusting.  And that's how she cured her scoliosis. And what was Katharina Schold's legacy ?  Catarina studied gymnastics, formalized the method she created herself, and opened an institute with her daughter, who became a physiotherapist.  For 60 years, they have healed people through movement and breathing.  [music] The medical community called her a fraud, but in the end she won a medal of honor.  Catherine revolutionized the treatment of scoliosis in Germany and the world. I'm Dr. Kelvin Ruan, I'm a physical therapist, and if you have scoliosis or know someone who does, stay until the end of this video because today you're going to learn a sequence of exercises. All of this is based on the same principles that Catarina used to cure her scoliosis, in addition, of course, to other tips that I will share with you that I have learned in these almost 10 years studying the spine.  Before we get to the exercises, let's first understand what scoliosis is.  Scoliosis creates a lateral curve in the spine, where one side of the trunk becomes more open, roughly speaking, and the other becomes more tense and shortened.  And the goal of the treatment is to lengthen the shortened side while strengthening the weak side.  Breathing combined with movement makes this happen .  And scoliosis has two fairly common causes, the first being entirely muscular, where the muscle actually begins to compress the spine on one side.  And another fairly common cause of scoliosis has to do with the bones in your leg.  What do you mean , doctor?  What does my leg have to do with my spine?  Look, this happens when one lower limb is shorter than the other.  In other words, the bone is short because it developed that way.  Imagine your body as if it were a house built on pillars.  The legs act like pillars, and if one pillar is lower than the other, the entire structure above begins to tilt, like a crooked house.  And that's exactly what happens to people with scoliosis.  Because the leg bones are directly connected to the pelvis, and the pelvis is connected to the spine, when one leg becomes shorter, that leg pulls that side of the pelvis downward.  And in this way, the spine tries to compensate for this unevenness automatically.  And how does she try to compensate?  It starts to bend.  At first, this curvature may be small and almost imperceptible, but over the years, especially after 50 or 60 years of age, the body suffers wear and tear because of it.  So that simple unevenness can develop into a more serious scoliosis, also associated with osteoarthritis, which is a wear and tear of the spinal bone itself, of the vertebrae of the spine.   In this scenario, the muscles become more tense, constantly trying to balance the body , the joints begin to overload, and, in addition, symptoms such as lower back pain, one shoulder higher than the other, joint wear and tear, etc., may appear.  Many people think it's just a problem in the spine, but in fact the origin can start down there, in the lower limbs.  There is a doctor's office test that you can do at home to find out if the cause of your scoliosis is in fact one leg being shorter than the other.  Obviously, this doesn't replace a professional evaluation, but it will give you a general idea of whether or not you need to see a doctor .  So look, if you have scoliosis, you're going to ask someone to take a picture of you.  Turn your back to that person and try to touch their feet with your hands, then ask them to take a picture of you in this position.  If one side of the back is higher than the other, it's a classic sign of scoliosis.  But doctor, how will I know if the cause of the scoliosis is a short leg, as you explained to me?  To find out , you're going to take this other test here.  Just look.  If you move from the navel to the sides, you will find the pelvic bones, which we call the iliac crest.  We have the left side and also the right side.  In front of the mirror, you should observe whether these two bones are at the same height, whether they are balanced or unbalanced, that is, one side higher than the other.  Another way to assess this is to lie on your stomach in bed and ask someone to take a picture of your feet.  The photo needs to look like this.  At this point, you observe whether your heels are aligned or if one is higher than the other, because that will mean that one leg is shorter, the leg with the higher heel.  This is a hypothesis that the pelvis is misaligned, shortening the leg.  And if I do this test on a patient and he presents with one leg shorter than the other, I ask him for a scanometry exam with angle analysis. Scoliosis is a mouthful, but basically in this exam the radiologist measures the size of the leg bones using X-rays and analyzes the degree of scoliosis.  And this examination confirms whether the cause of scoliosis is indeed the hypothesis of one leg being shorter than the other.
